summary.overall: Chapter 3 (Airport Traffic Control — General) has 178 agent-authored questions, 10 activities, and 146 flashcards across 94 paragraphs. Overall quality is adequate in operational substance but systematically undermined by two recurring defects: paragraph-location scaffolding (references to paragraph numbers in stems and flashcard fronts) and table-reference trivia (items that ask 'which table?' instead of the operational use of lighting and equipment). After remediation, 121 decisions were issued: 95 question replacements, 23 flashcard replacements, 1 activity replacements, 2 flashcard splits, and 0 removals. 17 decisions are major severity (primarily table-trivia replacements and overloaded flashcard splits). 104 decisions are minor (paragraph-reference removal, answer-position shuffling, answer-length balancing, and thin-content enrichment).
patterns: Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: approximately 90 question stems and 16 flashcard fronts reference paragraph numbers ('Under 3-1-1', 'According to 3-1-8'). These references have been systematically removed — the student needs to know the operational rule, not its paragraph number.

Table-reference trivia: 12 items in Section 3-4 (Airport Lighting) ask which table controls a lighting system instead of testing when and why the lighting is operated. These have been replaced with operational questions about the conditions and rationale for lighting use.
Answer-position bias: 12 multiple-choice items place the correct answer at position 0. Shuffled to distribute correct answers across positions 1-3.
Answer-length cues: 9 items have correct answers significantly longer than distractors (often 2x-3x the length). Answer texts have been balanced while preserving operational accuracy.

Overloaded flashcards: 2 flashcards (airport conditions list, BAA actions) have backs exceeding 290 characters that attempt to list all items from a multi-element rule. Split into focused paired cards.

Thin-content cards: 7 flashcards have backs of 10-11 characters that merely name a table or state a bare number without operational context. Replaced with cards that include the operational rationale.
Thin explanations: 3 question explanations are 21-64 characters and merely restate the answer. Enriched with operational reasoning about why the answer matters.

Fill_blank format limitation: Approximately 45 fill_blank items test single-word or short-phrase recall. Many are appropriate for phraseology and defined terms; a subset were converted to multiple_choice where the operational decision is richer than simple recall.
generation_guidance: NEVER embed paragraph numbers in question stems or flashcard fronts. Write self-contained items that test the operational rule, condition, or decision. The student studies the 7110.65 to learn air traffic procedures, not to index paragraphs.

NEVER ask 'which table' or 'which paragraph' questions. The operational skill is knowing WHAT to do under WHAT conditions, not WHERE to find it in the document. Replace all table-reference questions with scenario-based items about lighting operations.
Distribute correct answers across positions 1-4 (not 0-3) in multiple_choice items. Ensure no position has the correct answer more than 35% of the time within a chapter.
Balance distractor lengths to within 30% of the correct answer length. A conspicuously longer correct answer is a cue that undermines assessment validity.
Limit flashcard backs to approximately 200 characters for a single retrieval target. When a rule has multiple enumerated elements, split into paired cards grouping related items.

Flashcard backs must include operational rationale, not just bare facts. A card answering '20 minutes' to a question about ATIS wind shear duration is educationally hollow — add the operational reason.

Explanations must teach the controlling principle, condition, or exception — not restate the answer. A good explanation connects the correct choice to the operational context and explains why each distractor is wrong.

Use negative stems ('which is NOT...') only when exclusion recognition is the genuine operational skill (e.g., distinguishing what is NOT an unusual maneuver). Replace negative stems with positive stems when the same knowledge can be tested directly.

For Section 3-4 lighting paragraphs, focus items on the operational triggers and conditions for lighting use: when to turn on, what intensity to select, and why. Do not test which table number documents the settings.

Fill_blank format is appropriate for exact phraseology recall and defined terms (e.g., braking action terminology, minimum values). Use multiple_choice for operational decisions that involve judgment, conditions, and exceptions.
Ensure each generated item is independently answerable — the stem must contain sufficient context that a student does not need to look up a paragraph to understand what is being asked.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-1 note: traffic information can aid pilots but the pilot remains responsible for collision avoidance under the CFRs.

match.question_text: Which of the following best characterizes the relationship between ATC traffic information and the pilot's collision-avoidance responsibility under the 3-1-1 note?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following best characterizes the relationship between ATC traffic information and the pilot's collision-avoidance responsibility?
replacements.explanation: The note says traffic information 'can aid' pilots — it is helpful but does not replace or share the pilot's responsibility. The pilot 'is responsible' regardless of whether ATC provides traffic information.

choices.text: Traffic information aids pilots but does not shift or share the pilot's collision-avoidance responsibility.
Traffic information makes ATC solely responsible for collision avoidance once it is issued.
Traffic information and pilot responsibility are mutually exclusive — only one can apply at a time.
Traffic information creates a shared legal duty between the controller and the pilot.

choices.text: The controller's judgment that the observed abnormal condition makes an advisory necessary.
Approval from the facility supervisor before passing any observation.
Only a specific pilot request for an abnormality check.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-11: do not approve a speed in excess of 250 knots unless the pilot informs you a higher minimum speed is required; 14 CFR 91.117 permits speeds above 250 knots when required in the airplane flight manual.

match.question_text: A pilot requests to cross a Class D surface area at 220 knots. Traffic conditions permit. The controller is about to approve the request when the pilot adds that they'd also like to speed up to 275 knots because the flight manual recommends it. Under 3-1-11, what should the controller do?

replacements.question_text: A pilot requests to cross a Class D surface area at 220 knots. Traffic conditions permit. The controller is about to approve the request when the pilot adds that they'd also like to speed up to 275 knots because the flight manual recommends it. What should the controller do?
replacements.explanation: The paragraph allows exceeding the surface-area speed limit subject to the 250-knot cap unless the pilot informs the controller that a higher minimum speed is required. The pilot has now stated that the flight manual recommends the higher speed, which falls under the note's allowance. Since the pilot has informed the controller of the requirement, the 250-knot cap exception applies.

choices.text: Deny the crossing entirely because speeds above 250 knots are never allowed in surface areas.
Approve 275 knots because the pilot has stated the higher speed is required by the flight manual, which satisfies the exception.
Approve only 250 knots because the note does not mention flight manual recommendations as a valid exception.
Approve 220 knots only and instruct the pilot to accelerate after leaving the surface area.

A touch-and-go landing conducted under tower instruction.
An unnecessary low pass over the field.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-12: ground control must assist local control in visually scanning runways, especially when runways are in close proximity to other movement areas.

match.question_text: A tower has runways that are widely separated with clear sight lines and no movement areas between them. Under 3-1-12, how does the ground controller's scanning obligation differ from a tower where runways are very close to active taxiways?

replacements.question_text: A tower has runways that are widely separated with clear sight lines and no movement areas between them. How does the ground controller's scanning obligation differ from a tower where runways are very close to active taxiways?
replacements.explanation: Ground must always assist, but the paragraph singles out 'especially when runways are in close proximity to other movement areas' as the situation where the assisting role is most critical. Both situations require assistance, but the emphasis varies.

choices.text: Ground's scanning obligation is identical regardless of the runway layout or proximity to movement areas.
Ground must assist in both cases, but the obligation is especially emphasized when runways are close to other movement areas.
Ground must assist only when runways are close to other movement areas; otherwise ground has no scanning duty.
With widely separated runways, ground must take over primary scanning duty from local.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-13: responding with the aircraft call sign establishes two-way communications; the controller must use 'REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY' to deny entry.

match.question_text: A pilot calls the tower on initial contact and the controller responds with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.' The controller is then distracted by an emergency and cannot provide services for several minutes. The pilot, having heard 'standby,' enters the Class D. Under 3-1-13, is the pilot's entry permitted?

replacements.question_text: A pilot calls the tower on initial contact and the controller responds with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.' The controller is then distracted by an emergency and cannot provide services for several minutes. The pilot, having heard 'standby,' enters the Class D. Is the pilot's entry permitted?
replacements.explanation: The paragraph says that when the controller responds with the call sign and 'standby,' communications are established and the pilot CAN enter. However, if workload prevents immediate service provision, the controller must tell the pilot to REMAIN OUTSIDE. In this scenario, the controller said only 'standby' — which does establish communications and legally permits entry — but the controller should have used the 'remain outside' instruction to keep the pilot out. Since the controller only said 'standby,' the pilot's entry was technically permitted.

choices.text: No, because any gap in controller attention automatically revokes the prior communication establishment.
No, because the controller intended to delay services, which implicitly means the pilot must remain outside.
Yes, because 'standby' with the call sign established two-way communications, which is all that is required for Class D entry.
Yes, but only if the pilot first requests and receives an explicit 'enter the Delta' clearance.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-14: provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures to the extent possible when volcanic ash is present, to minimize re-suspension of ash.

match.question_text: A departing aircraft at an ash-contaminated airport is holding in position on the runway. The controller normally uses a 'cleared for takeoff' call that involves a brief pause after the aircraft lines up. Under 3-1-14, what should the controller do differently?

replacements.question_text: A departing aircraft at an ash-contaminated airport is holding in position on the runway. The controller normally uses a 'cleared for takeoff' call that involves a brief pause after the aircraft lines up. What should the controller do differently?
replacements.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers to 'provide for a rolling takeoff for all departures' to the extent possible when volcanic ash is present, because stopping and restarting on ash surfaces makes large amounts of ash airborne again.

choices.text: Use standard procedures — volcanic ash on the surface does not change takeoff clearance procedures.
Issue the takeoff clearance so the aircraft can begin its roll without stopping, to the extent possible.
Require the aircraft to hold in position longer than usual to verify ash clearance before takeoff.
Cancel all departures until the ash has been completely removed from the runway surface.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-15: controller response options are to provide service as soon as operationally practical, advise of delay and expected duration, or advise that a significant disruption prevents service.

match.question_text: A PIC requests to return to the gate under the Tarmac Rule. The controller issues 'EXPECT A 15 MINUTE DELAY DUE TO DEPARTING TRAFFIC.' The pilot accepts the delay but calls back 20 minutes later still waiting. Under 3-1-15, what response options does the controller now have?

replacements.question_text: A PIC requests to return to the gate under the Tarmac Rule. The controller issues 'EXPECT A 15 MINUTE DELAY DUE TO DEPARTING TRAFFIC.' The pilot accepts the delay but calls back 20 minutes later still waiting. What response options does the controller now have?
replacements.explanation: The paragraph's two-part response applies on each request: provide service as soon as operationally practical, or advise of significant disruption. The controller may assess whether conditions have changed since the DELAY advisory and apply either response again. The DELAY phraseology is one of three options the controller can use.

choices.text: Once a delay advisory is issued, the controller may not switch to an unable response for the same request.
Re-evaluate whether to provide the return service or advise of significant disruption — the expect delay advisory is just one of the available response options.
The expect delay advisory commits the controller to providing service after the stated time without further assessment.
The pilot must make a new request under different wording to receive a different response.

operations.source_basis: 3-1-2: provide preventive control service only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement or DoD equivalent.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft not operating under any letter of agreement requests that the tower use 'preventive control' to reduce radio calls during a long pattern session. Is this permissible?
replacements.explanation: The paragraph says 'Provide preventive control service only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement' (or DoD equivalents per the note). It is not available by pilot request alone.

choices.text: Yes — but only during VFR conditions and when the tower is not busy.
Yes — any pilot can request preventive control to reduce frequency congestion.
No — preventive control service requires an LOA or its DoD equivalent; it cannot be provided on pilot request alone.
No — unless the pilot also files a written request with the facility at least 24 hours in advance.

replacements.question_text: An airport vehicle with an LOA and two-way communications requests to operate in the RSA near Runway 18 while aircraft are arriving. What is the controller's correct response?

match.question_text: A controller issues an alternating red-and-green warning signal to an aircraft for a soft-field condition. The pilot, seeing red in the signal, comes to a complete stop and refuses to move. The pilot later argues that any signal containing red means 'stop.' Is the pilot's interpretation correct under 3-2-2?

replacements.question_text: A controller issues an alternating red-and-green warning signal to an aircraft for a soft-field condition. The pilot, seeing red in the signal, comes to a complete stop and refuses to move. The pilot later argues that any signal containing red means 'stop.' Is the pilot's interpretation correct?
replacements.explanation: The note explicitly states the warning signal is 'not a prohibitive signal.' It is a caution that means 'exercise extreme caution,' not 'stop.' It can even be followed by another light signal. The pilot should exercise heightened alertness, not stop.

choices.text: Yes — any red component in an ATC light signal always requires the aircraft to stop.
No — the warning signal is not prohibitive; it means exercise extreme caution, and the aircraft may continue with heightened alertness.
Yes — the warning signal is prohibitive and means the aircraft must not move until a different signal is given.
No — but the aircraft should return to the starting point before continuing.

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three listed conditions for directing the alternating red-and-green general warning signal?
replacements.explanation: The three listed conditions are: (a) converging aircraft with collision hazard, (b) mechanical trouble unknown to pilot, (c) other hazardous conditions requiring intensified alertness. 'Pilot requests a radio check' is not listed and would be handled through radio, not light signals.

choices.text: The pilot requests a radio check to verify communication quality.
Aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists.
Mechanical trouble exists of which the pilot might not be aware.
Other hazardous conditions are present which call for intensified pilot or operator alertness.

replacements.question_text: A receiver-only helicopter is in flight at night and needs to acknowledge a light signal. The pilot flashes the navigation lights. Is this correct?
replacements.explanation: For helicopters between sunset and sunrise, the approved acknowledgment is to 'Flash landing light or search light.' Navigation lights are the nighttime acknowledgment for fixed-wing aircraft, not helicopters. This is a discrimination point between aircraft categories.

choices.text: No — helicopters at night must flash the landing light or search light, not navigation lights.
Yes — all aircraft use navigation or landing lights at night for acknowledgment.
Yes — but only if the landing light is inoperative.
No — helicopters at night must rock the tip path plane instead.

replacements.question_text: What is the key operational difference between the daylight acknowledgment for a hovering helicopter versus a helicopter in flight?
replacements.explanation: A hovering helicopter may either 'turn the helicopter toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light or rock the tip path plane.' A helicopter in flight may 'either flash the landing light or rock the tip path plane.' The hovering procedure adds the option of turning toward the facility as part of the acknowledgment.

choices.text: There is no difference — hovering and in-flight helicopters use identical acknowledgments.
Hovering helicopters must rock the wings; in-flight helicopters must flash the landing light.
In-flight helicopters may move ailerons or rudder; hovering helicopters cannot.
Hovering helicopters have the additional option of turning toward the controlling facility while flashing the landing light; in-flight helicopters do not have the turn option.

choices.text: Yes — ATIS is the designated distribution system and verbal issuance is optional.
No — the updated RwyCC must be verbally issued to aircraft until the ATIS can be updated.

operations.source_basis: 3-4-11: high intensity runway lights and associated centerline and touchdown zone lights are operated based on time of day and visibility conditions to provide adequate runway environment visibility.

match.question_text: High intensity runway and associated centerline and touchdown zone lights are operated in accordance with which table?

replacements.question_text: High intensity runway lights (HIRL) and associated centerline and touchdown zone lights are operated based on what operational factors?
replacements.explanation: HIRL intensity settings are determined by time of day (day/night/twilight) and reported visibility or RVR. Higher settings are used in daytime and low visibility; lower settings at night to prevent glare. The controller's operational decision is based on conditions, not on memorizing table numbers.

choices.text: The pilot's specific request for a particular lighting intensity setting.
The aircraft's weight category and type of approach being flown.
Whether the runway has a precision or non-precision approach procedure.
Time of day (day/night/twilight) and reported visibility or RVR.

operations.problem: Fill_blank question asks which table controls HIRL associated with MALSR. Tests document-navigation trivia.
operations.source_basis: 3-4-12: HIRL that control the associated MALSR must be operated at coordinated intensities so approach lighting remains visible relative to runway edge lighting during final approach.

match.question_text: HIRL that control the associated MALSR are operated in accordance with which table?

replacements.question_text: Why is it operationally important to coordinate HIRL intensity settings with the associated MALSR?
replacements.explanation: MALSR supplements HIRL during approaches. The two must be operated at coordinated intensities so the approach lighting system remains clearly visible relative to the runway edge lighting throughout the final approach segment. Mismatched intensities can degrade the pilot's visual reference.

choices.text: HIRL and MALSR are independent systems with no operational relationship.
HIRL must always be set to maximum intensity whenever MALSR is operating.
MALSR intensity always automatically controls HIRL intensity — no coordination is needed.
Coordinated intensities ensure the approach lighting remains visible relative to runway edge lighting on final.

operations.problem: Fill_blank question asks which table controls MIRL operation. Answer 'TBL 3-4-10' tests trivia. Also has thin explanation (21 chars).

match.question_text: MIRL or MIRL which control associated MALSR are operated in accordance with which table?

replacements.question_text: What is the primary operational difference between Medium Intensity Runway Lights (MIRL) and High Intensity Runway Lights (HIRL) during periods of reduced visibility?
replacements.explanation: MIRL systems have a lower maximum intensity than HIRL systems. During very low visibility conditions, MIRL may not provide adequate visual guidance for pilots, whereas HIRL can be set to higher intensity steps. MIRL is typically adequate for better visibility conditions.

choices.text: MIRL can achieve a higher maximum intensity than HIRL in all conditions.
There is no operational difference — the same settings apply to both lighting systems.
MIRL has a lower maximum intensity and may not provide adequate guidance in the lowest visibility conditions.
MIRL is used only for taxiway lighting and is never installed on runways.

operations.problem: Multiple choice question asks which table controls PAPI intensity. Tests table-number trivia, not operational use.

match.question_text: PAPI systems with remote on-off switching must be operated when they serve the runway in use and intensities are controlled in accordance with which table?

replacements.question_text: PAPI systems with remote on-off switching must be operated when they serve the runway in use. What determines the appropriate PAPI intensity setting?
replacements.explanation: PAPI intensity is set based on time of day (day/twilight/night) to ensure the visual glidepath indication is clearly visible without causing glare. Higher intensity is needed during daytime to overcome ambient light; lower settings at night prevent dazzling pilots.

choices.text: PAPI intensity is fixed and cannot be adjusted by ATC once installed.
The pilot's aircraft type and approach category determine the PAPI setting.
Time of day — day, twilight, or night — determines the appropriate intensity setting.
The type of approach procedure — ILS, RNAV, or visual — determines the PAPI setting.

replacements.explanation: Approach Lighting System (ALS) intensity settings are based on time of day (day/twilight/night) and prevailing visibility. The approach lighting must provide adequate visual guidance to the runway environment throughout the final approach segment without causing glare or distraction.

choices.text: The number of aircraft in the approach sequence at the time.
The specific category of ILS approach (CAT I, II, or III) in use.
Airport classification — commercial service airports use higher settings than general aviation airports.

replacements.explanation: The note explains that the requirements of paragraph 5-3-1 (Application) cannot be assured — specifically 'continued positive radar identification' would place 'distracting and operationally inefficient requirements upon the local controller.' choices.text: Because tower radar displays are intended only for ground movement surveillance and cannot track airborne aircraft.

operations.source_basis: 3-3-7e: when FFM alarm sounds and aircraft is outside the MM, direct vehicle/personnel to clear the critical area; if alarm persists, issue unreliable-localizer advisory.

match.question_text: The FFM alarm sounds while an aircraft is outside the middle marker. The controller checks and sees a vehicle in the critical area. What should the controller do?

replacements.question_text: The FFM alarm sounds while an aircraft is outside the middle marker. The controller checks and sees a vehicle in the critical area. What should the controller do?
replacements.explanation: The controller should direct the vehicle to clear the critical area immediately. The paragraph says to 'check for encroachment of those portions of the critical area that can be seen from the tower' to 'determine possible causal factors.' If encroachment is found, clearing it may resolve the alarm. If the alarm persists until the MM/1/2 mile final, the advisory is then issued.

choices.text: Immediately issue the unreliable-localizer advisory without taking any action regarding the critical area vehicle.
Direct the vehicle to clear the critical area; if alarm persists past the MM, issue the unreliable-localizer advisory.
Ignore the alarm entirely because the cause is known to be vehicle encroachment rather than equipment malfunction.
Cancel all approaches immediately — any critical-area encroachment automatically invalidates the localizer signal.

operations.problem: Flashcard back (294 chars) lists all eight airport condition categories, making it a multi-rule dump beyond a single retrieval target. Split into two focused cards grouping related conditions.
operations.source_basis: 3-3-3: airport condition information includes construction, rough areas, braking conditions, snow, parked aircraft, navaid irregularities, volcanic ash, and other safety-affecting conditions.

match.front: List the eight categories of airport condition information in 3-3-3.
match.back: 1) Construction on/near movement area. 2) Rough movement area portions. 3) Braking conditions (ice, snow, slush, water). 4) Snowdrifts/piles and plowed extent. 5) Parked aircraft on movement area. 6) Irregular airport lighting. 7) Volcanic ash (wet/dry if known). 8) Other pertinent conditions.

replacements.front: What surface-related conditions must be included in airport condition information?
replacements.back: 1) Construction on or near the movement area. 2) Rough portions of the movement area. 3) Braking conditions (ice, snow, slush, water). 4) Snowdrifts, snow piles, and the extent of plowing on the movement area.

replacements.front: What hazard and operational status conditions must be included in airport condition information?
replacements.back: 1) Parked aircraft on the movement area. 2) Irregular operation of navigational aids. 3) Volcanic ash on the airport (state whether wet or dry — wet ash significantly reduces braking). 4) Any other airport condition affecting safe operations.

operations.problem: Flashcard back (328 chars) lists all four Braking Action Advisory actions. Overloaded for single retrieval. Split into two focused cards.
operations.source_basis: 3-3-5: during BAA, controllers must issue braking reports, include BAA on ATIS, advise Airport Operator of medium/poor/nil reports, and issue NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS when none exist.

match.front: What four actions must be taken during Braking Action Advisories?
match.back: 1) Issue latest braking report to each arriving and departing aircraft (prefer same weight class for super/heavy). 2) If no report exists for the runway, issue NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS advisory. 3) Advise the Airport Operator of degraded braking reports (good to medium through nil). 4) Solicit PIREPs of runway braking action.

replacements.front: What must be issued to each arriving and departing aircraft during Braking Action Advisories?
replacements.back: The latest braking action report for the runway. For super or heavy aircraft, prefer issuing a report from a similar weight class. If no report exists for the runway, issue: 'NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS RECEIVED FOR RUNWAY (number).'

replacements.front: What administrative and notification actions are required during Braking Action Advisories?
replacements.back: 1) Include Braking Action Advisories on the ATIS broadcast. 2) Advise the Airport Operator when braking action reports include 'medium,' 'poor,' or 'nil.'

replacements.front: How long must a wind shear/microburst statement remain on the ATIS after the last report, and why?

content.situation: An updated RwyCC report arrives for Runway 9: the codes have changed from 5-5-5 to 3-3-3. Verbally issue the updated codes to all arriving and departing aircraft until the ATIS is updated.
Issue the update only to arriving aircraft because departures do not need RwyCC.
Issue the update only if a pilot directly asks about runway conditions.

content.explanation: 3-3-1E(1)(b) explicitly requires verbal issuance of RwyCC updates to aircraft until the ATIS broadcast can be updated.
